title: Treating garden greywater with a simple constructed wetland
subtitle: How a shallow planted bed filters laundry and washing-up water for garden reuse
excerpt: Greywater from a kitchen or laundry can be diverted to a constructed wetland: a shallow lined bed filled with gravel and planted with wetland species. Plants and the gravel biofilm remove suspended solids, phosphates, and pathogens. The treated water drains to a soakaway or a second storage tank for irrigation.
sourceNotes: Constructed Wetlands for Water Quality Improvement, Moshiri (ed.). CIRIA Report C753 on SuDS (Sustainable Drainage Systems) and constructed wetlands. Environment Agency guidance on greywater reuse. Scottish Water greywater guidance.

content.text: is sized at about 2 to 5 m2 of bed per person, set by how much water the house puts out. A single-person system for laundry only (one or two washes a week) does fine with a 2 m by 1 m bed. A full house, with bath and shower water as well, needs a bigger bed.
Excavate a bed 400-500 mm deep. Line with a heavy-duty pond liner (0.5 mm LDPE or EPDM). Overlap the liner at the edges and fold over the rim.
Fill with pea gravel or washed 10-20 mm gravel to within 50-75 mm of the top. The inlet end should be slightly higher than the outlet end (10-15 mm per metre) to encourage flow.

Fit the inlet pipe along the inlet end. Use 50 mm perforated pipe at the gravel surface. Fit the outlet pipe at the far end. Use 50 mm pipe at the base of the gravel, with a standpipe you can move up and down to set the water level.

Plant with wetland species: common reed (Phragmites australis), yellow iris (Iris pseudacorus), or reedmace (Typha latifolia). Space at 300-400 mm centres. The plants establish over one growing season.

Phosphate detergents speed up algae growth in rivers and ponds. Use phosphate-free laundry and dish products if the treated water will drain to a watercourse or to a soakaway near one. Plant-based and low-salt detergents are kinder to soil life if the treated water goes straight on the garden.

In England and Wales, reusing treated greywater for garden irrigation from a domestic constructed wetland is generally permitted without a licence provided the water does not enter a watercourse and the system does not include blackwater (toilet waste). If the treated water drains to a ditch or watercourse, a permit from the Environment Agency may be required. Check the current guidance before connecting the outlet.
